title: Gateau Breton
subtitle: A dense, buttery Breton cake with a shortbread-like crumb
excerpt: Gateau Breton is a regional French cake from Brittany: thick, very buttery, and dense in a way that lies somewhere between cake and rich shortbread. It contains little besides flour, butter, sugar, and egg yolks, and owes its character entirely to the quality and quantity of the butter.
sourceNotes: Gateau Breton is a traditional regional cake of Brittany, France, documented in French regional cookbooks from the late nineteenth century. The standard form uses only flour, butter, sugar, and egg yolks; the recipe here follows the established home-baking tradition now in the public domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, wrap tightly, and freeze. Thaw at room temperature for 2 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps well at room temperature for 4 to 5 days, covered. The flavour and texture improve after a day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 160 degrees C; the top should be deep golden with a crosshatch impression

glossaryTerms.definition: The texture of gateau Breton is unlike most cakes: dense, short, and crumbly rather than light and sponge-like, because it contains a very high proportion of butter relative to flour and no liquid beyond egg yolks. The crumb is rich and slightly sandy, and slices cleanly only when the cake is fully cooled. The absence of leavening is deliberate: the cake's mass is part of its character.

content.text: Gateau Breton is a very thick, very rich shortbread baked as a cake. The ratio of butter to flour is extremely high; the dough is mixed by creaming rather than rubbing, and the result is dense and sliceable rather than crumbly or dry. The
is the point of the cake: not lightness or rise, but a deep buttery richness that improves with a day's rest. The crosshatch pattern on top is traditional and helps identify the top crust once baked.

items.prepNote: at room temperature, plus 1 more for egg wash

content.text: Preheat the oven to 180 degrees C (conventional). Grease and line the base of the tin. Beat the butter and sugar together for 3 minutes until pale. Add 4 of the egg yolks one at a time, beating after each. Add the salt and the rum or vanilla. Fold in the flour until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms. It will look more like a very thick batter than a standard cake dough.

Tip the dough into the prepared tin and press it out to an even layer about 2 to 3 cm deep. Use a wet palette knife or the back of a spoon to smooth the top. Beat the remaining egg yolk with a teaspoon of water and brush it over the surface. Use a fork to draw a crosshatch pattern lightly across the top. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes, until the top is deep golden and a skewer comes out clean. The sides will have pulled slightly from the tin. Cool in the tin for 30 minutes, then unmould and leave on a rack for at least another 30 minutes before slicing. It cuts cleanly only when fully cool.
